name: Embalse de Santa Teresa and Ermita de Valdejimena — circular by the Río Tormes
name_original: Embalse de Santa Teresa y Ermita de Valdejimena — circular por el Río Tormes
region_intro: No traffic road cycling routes around Embalse de Santa Teresa traverse a landscape characterized by gently rolling hills, adorned with oak and almond trees, and interspersed with scrublands and pastures. The reservoir itself provides expansive views, often referred to as an "inland sea," with the distant Sierra de Béjar visible on the horizon. This region offers varied terrain for road cyclists, including both paved surfaces and segments with elevation gains. Best no traffic road cycling routes around Embalse de Santa Teresa The most popular no traffic road cycling route is Roadbike loop from Salvatierra de Tormes, a 17.9 miles (28.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 14 minutes to complete. This easy route offers scenic views of the surrounding countryside. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Guijuelo Jamón District – Climb Towards the Reservoir loop from Antigua Estación de Guijuelo, a moderate 32.6 miles (52.4 km) path. This route features significant elevation changes, providing a rewarding challenge. Local road cyclists also love the Puerto del Tremedal – Puerto del Tremedal loop from La Cabeza de Béjar, a 32.7 miles (52.7 km) trail leading through challenging terrain with over 1000 meters of elevation gain, often completed in about 2 hours 53 minutes. Road cycling around Embalse de Santa Teresa is defined by rolling hills, oak forests, and expansive reservoir views, with distant mountains adding to the scenic backdrop. The network offers options for various ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging climbs. The routes in Embalse de Santa Teresa are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 10 reviews. More than 800 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Embalse de Santa Teresa's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many no-traffic road cycling routes are available around Embalse de Santa Teresa?
A: There are 6 dedicated no-traffic road cycling routes around Embalse de Santa Teresa listed on komoot, catering to various skill levels from easy to difficult.

Q: What is the general terrain like for road cycling in the Embalse de Santa Teresa region?
A: The region is characterized by gently rolling hills, offering a mix of ascents and descents. You'll cycle through landscapes adorned with oak and almond trees, scrublands, and lush pastures. While most routes feature paved surfaces, some segments might be unpaved and more challenging.

Q: Are there any easy no-traffic road cycling routes suitable for beginners or a relaxed ride?
A: Yes, there are a couple of easy routes perfect for beginners or those seeking a relaxed pace. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24540907" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roadbike loop from Salvatierra de Tormes&lt;/a&gt; is a great option, covering approximately 28.8 km with moderate elevation gain.

Q: What kind of views and natural features can I expect to see along these routes?
A: Cyclists can enjoy panoramic views of the Embalse de Santa Teresa, often referred to as an 'inland sea,' with the distant Sierra de Béjar providing a stunning backdrop. The area is also a significant ecological spot, where you might observe migratory birds, including cranes, in a tranquil natural setting.

Q: Are there more challenging no-traffic road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists looking for a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38197147" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto del Tremedal – Puerto del Tremedal loop from La Cabeza de Béjar&lt;/a&gt; offer significant climbs and cover over 52 km with substantial elevation gain, providing a rewarding experience.

Q: Are there any circular no-traffic road cycling routes around Embalse de Santa Teresa?
A: Yes, many of the routes are circular, allowing you to start and end at the same point. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20142546" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Guijuelo Jamón District – Climb Towards the Reservoir loop from Antigua Estación de Guijuelo&lt;/a&gt;, which is a moderate 52 km ride.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Embalse de Santa Teresa?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars. Reviewers often praise the diverse landscapes, the scenic beauty of the reservoir, and the varied terrain that provides both physical challenge and picturesque views, especially on the low-traffic roads.

Q: Are there any specific points of interest or attractions near the no-traffic cycling routes?
A: Beyond the natural beauty of the reservoir and its surroundings, you can explore the historical town of Salvatierra de Tormes. The wider area also features cultural points of interest, such as the Granite Theme Park in Los Santos, which highlights the local stonemasonry tradition.

Q: What is the best time of year to go road cycling around Embalse de Santa Teresa?
A: The region offers pleasant cycling conditions for much of the year. Spring and autumn generally provide milder temperatures and beautiful scenery with blooming flora or vibrant fall colors. Summer can be warm, so early morning or late afternoon rides are recommended during peak heat.

Q: Is public transport available to reach the cycling routes around Embalse de Santa Teresa?
A: While specific public transport links directly to all route starting points might be limited, major towns in the vicinity, such as Guijuelo or Salvatierra de Tormes, may have bus connections. It's advisable to check local transport schedules for the most up-to-date information if you plan to use public transport.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a no-traffic road cycling route?
A: Many of the routes originate from or pass through towns like Salvatierra de Tormes or Guijuelo. These towns typically offer public parking facilities. For routes starting from more rural points, look for designated parking areas or safe roadside spots, always ensuring not to obstruct local access.
